INNER JOIN public.courses c

visualization.title: Courses that are prerequisites for the most other courses

axis.title: Number of courses that list this as a prerequisite

resultRaw.sql:
SELECT p.prerequisite_course_id AS pr_course_id, c.code AS pr_course_code, c.name AS pr_course_name, COUNT(DISTINCT p.course_id) AS dependent_count
FROM public.prerequisites p
INNER JOIN public.courses c
  ON p.prerequisite_course_id = c.id
GROUP BY p.prerequisite_course_id, c.code, c.name
ORDER BY dependent_count DESC, pr_course_code ASC;

issues: All encoded fields (pr_course_code, pr_course_name, dependent_count) exist in metadata.columns — good.
The SQL performs aggregation (one row per prerequisite course with COUNT DISTINCT); the chart uses the aggregated dependent_count directly — mapping is consistent.

Vega-Lite sorts x by dependent_count descending, but the SQL also specified a secondary ORDER BY pr_course_code ASC; Vega-Lite does not include the secondary tie-breaker, so ties may be ordered differently than the SQL result.

pr_course_id (the primary key) is present in the SQL output but not used in the visualization; if pr_course_code is not unique across courses, multiple distinct pr_course_id rows could share the same pr_course_code and should be disambiguated (this is a potential ambiguity, not a blocker).

The plan suggested possibly limiting to top N (e.g., top 20) for large result sets; the SQL has no LIMIT — if the result is large the chart may become crowded. Consider applying a LIMIT or filtering in the query or visualization.

Color encodes dependent_count without a legend (legend:null). This is acceptable but may reduce interpretability; consider adding a legend or a continuous color scale label if color is intended to convey magnitude.

visualization.title: Courses that are prerequisites for the most other courses

axis.title: Number of courses that list this as a prerequisite

result.sql:
SELECT p.prerequisite_course_id AS pr_course_id, c.code AS pr_course_code, c.name AS pr_course_name, COUNT(DISTINCT p.course_id) AS dependent_count
FROM public.prerequisites p
INNER JOIN public.courses c
  ON p.prerequisite_course_id = c.id
GROUP BY p.prerequisite_course_id, c.code, c.name
ORDER BY dependent_count DESC, pr_course_code ASC;

issues: All encoded fields (pr_course_code, pr_course_name, dependent_count) exist in metadata.columns — good.
The SQL performs aggregation (one row per prerequisite course with COUNT DISTINCT); the chart uses the aggregated dependent_count directly — mapping is consistent.

Vega-Lite sorts x by dependent_count descending, but the SQL also specified a secondary ORDER BY pr_course_code ASC; Vega-Lite does not include the secondary tie-breaker, so ties may be ordered differently than the SQL result.

pr_course_id (the primary key) is present in the SQL output but not used in the visualization; if pr_course_code is not unique across courses, multiple distinct pr_course_id rows could share the same pr_course_code and should be disambiguated (this is a potential ambiguity, not a blocker).

The plan suggested possibly limiting to top N (e.g., top 20) for large result sets; the SQL has no LIMIT — if the result is large the chart may become crowded. Consider applying a LIMIT or filtering in the query or visualization.

Color encodes dependent_count without a legend (legend:null). This is acceptable but may reduce interpretability; consider adding a legend or a continuous color scale label if color is intended to convey magnitude.
